does: This paper investigates backdoor vulnerabilities in instruction-tuned large language models by demonstrating that attackers can inject malicious instructions (~1000 tokens) into training data to control model behavior through data poisoning. The authors achieve over 90% attack success rates across four NLP datasets without modifying individual data instances or labels. They systematically evaluate instruction attacks across multiple dimensions: poison transfer showing backdoors transfer to 15 diverse datasets in zero-shot settings, instruction transfer enabling direct application of poisoned instructions across datasets, and poison resistance under continual finetuning. The study also examines potential mitigations including RLHF and clean demonstrations, finding they provide limited protection against these attacks.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in backdoor attacks and data poisoning, extending earlier studies on neural network vulnerabilities to the emerging instruction tuning paradigm. It connects to recent work on safety vulnerabilities in large language models and concerns about crowdsourced data quality in transformer training. The paper responds to the rapid adoption of instruction-tuning methods like those used in GPT-3.5 and similar models, where task instructions have become crucial for model behavior. It also relates to prior research on prompt injection and adversarial examples in language models, but focuses specifically on the training-time attack surface that instruction tuning creates, building on theoretical frameworks for understanding backdoor mechanisms in deep learning.
enables: This work establishes instruction attacks as a critical security concern for deployed instruction-tuned models, pointing toward the need for robust defense mechanisms and verification procedures in crowdsourced training pipelines. It opens research directions into detecting poisoned instructions, designing instruction tuning methods resilient to such attacks, and understanding fundamental properties that make instructions effective attack vectors. The findings motivate development of better authentication and validation methods for instruction data, formal guarantees about model robustness to instruction poisoning, and enhanced RLHF and alignment techniques that can certifiably eliminate backdoors. This research also highlights the importance of understanding supply-chain security in large-scale model training, with implications for responsible deployment of instruction-tuned systems in safety-critical applications.
